root_causes.cause: Software timestamping instead of hardware (adds variable processing delay)
QoS deprioritizing OAM frames (queuing delay added to measurement)
NTP/PTP clock not synchronized for one-way delay (DMM one-way requires precise clock sync)

symptoms: Reported delay/loss values don't match expected baseline
One-way delay shows negative or implausible values

failure_modes.description: A single connectivity fault generates multiple alarms across service layers because AIS is not properly suppressing client-layer notifications

Enable AIS generation on the detecting MEP (server layer)
Enable AIS generation on the detecting MEP (server layer)
Configure client MEPs to accept AIS at the expected MD level

Verify MD level hierarchy — AIS must be sent at the client MD level

root_causes.cause: AIS not enabled on server-layer MEP
Client-layer MEP not configured to receive AIS
MD level hierarchy prevents AIS propagation (AIS sent at wrong level)

symptoms: Multiple alarms raised for single fault
Alarm storm in NMS from cascading service failures
Client-layer MEPs report defect instead of AIS suppression

messages.description: Periodic multicast message for continuity verification
messages.name: CCM (Continuity Check Message)
messages.when_sent: Every CCM interval while MEP is active

messages.description: L2 traceroute equivalent — hop-by-hop through MIPs
messages.name: LTM/LTR (Linktrace Message/Reply)

messages.description: Y.1731 — sent when MEP detects CCM loss, suppresses alarms at client layer
messages.name: AIS (Alarm Indication Signal)
messages.when_sent: Automatically generated upon detecting connectivity fault to suppress downstream alarms

messages.description: Y.1731 — indicates administrative lock for maintenance
messages.name: LCK (Locked Signal)
messages.when_sent: Sent during administrative maintenance window to signal locked condition

messages.description: Y.1731 — one-way and two-way frame delay measurement
messages.name: DMM/DMR (Delay Measurement Message/Reply)
messages.when_sent: Periodically or on demand for delay SLA monitoring

messages.description: Y.1731 — near-end and far-end frame loss measurement
messages.name: LMM/LMR (Loss Measurement Message/Reply)
messages.when_sent: Periodically for loss SLA monitoring — counts data frames between measurement points

messages.description: Y.1731 — synthetic frame injection for loss measurement without data traffic counting
messages.name: SLM/SLR (Synthetic Loss Message/Reply)
messages.when_sent: Periodically — uses synthetic test frames instead of counting actual data traffic

purpose: Carrier Ethernet OAM — fault management (CFM) and performance monitoring (Y.1731)

states.description: MEP enabled and sending CCMs, monitoring for faults
states.name: MEP_ACTIVE

states.description: MEP has detected a fault condition (one or more defects active)
states.name: MEP_DEFECT

states.description: Remote Defect Indication received from peer MEP
states.name: RDI_RECEIVED

timers.name: CCM Interval
timers.tuning_advice: 1s is standard for fault detection. Use 3.3ms or 10ms for fast protection switching (G.8032). Detection time = 3.5x CCM interval (e.g., 1s interval = 3.5s detection).

timers.description: Time to wait for LTR responses
timers.name: Linktrace Reply Timeout
timers.on_expiry: Linktrace operation completes with partial results
timers.tuning_advice: Increase for large or congested L2 networks where LTR may be delayed

timers.name: Y.1731 PM Measurement Interval
timers.tuning_advice: 100ms-1s for real-time SLA monitoring. Longer intervals for baseline trending. Align with SLA reporting period.

transitions.failure_if_stuck: Persistent connectivity fault — check physical path, MAID/level configuration, and VLAN mapping end-to-end
transitions.trigger: CCM loss detected (3.5x CCM interval with no CCM from peer), cross-connect CCM received, or error CCM received
All defect conditions cleared (CCM received from expected peer, no mismatches)
CCM received with RDI flag set (peer MEP has detected a defect)
CCM received without RDI flag (peer defect cleared)

notes.note: IOS-XE full CFM + Y.1731 support on ASR 1000/9000, Catalyst 8000. 'ethernet cfm domain' for MD configuration. Y.1731 PM with hardware-assisted timestamps. 'show ethernet cfm' hierarchy. Supports CFM over pseudowire.

IOS-XR CFM fully supported on ASR 9000, NCS 5500. Y.1731 with hardware timestamps. 'ethernet cfm domain' config. Up MEPs and down MEPs. Supports SLA-based PM probes under 'ethernet sla'. 'show ethernet cfm peer meps' for MEP database.

NX-OS supports CFM on Nexus 7000 series (limited on 9000). 'feature cfm' required. MEPs configured under 'ethernet cfm domain' hierarchy. Y.1731 performance monitoring supported with hardware timestamps on select linecards. 'show ethernet cfm' commands.

CFM/Y.1731 is a core feature of SAOS — every carrier Ethernet service typically has CFM MEPs. Hardware-assisted timestamping on all platforms. MEPs tied to MEF Flow Points. Subject-verb CLI: 'cfm show mep' not 'show ethernet cfm'. Supports sub-second CCM intervals (3.3ms, 10ms) for G.8032 integration. Full Y.1731 suite: DMM, LMM, SLM, AIS, LCK. SAT (Service Activation Testing) uses Y.1731 for throughput/latency/loss testing per MEF 48.
